text: The PRT concept has been heavily criticized by NGOs staff in Afghanistan since the very beginning. They assert that the presence of a military component in a supposedly aid-related endeavour has the effect of confusing the insurgency, and thereby preventing it from distinguishing between military and non-military personnel. Some media outlets have reiterated on numerous occasions that, as a result, aid personnel would probably be more exposed to attacks. As this assertion has been done time and again in the press without much empirical underpinning, this research aimed at empirically checking the “exposure” to attacks in the case of NGOs staff in provinces where a PRT was stationed. The research applies a panel-corrected standard error regression model for all 34 Afghan provinces for 2010 and 2011. Preliminary findings suggest that there was a statistically significant presence of attacks on NGOs in PRT stations. Yet, further investigation shows that this was the case in PRTs commanded by any military beside the U.S. Army, therefore questioning the applicability of the “blurred lines” argument, and the assertion that aid personnel were likely to be attacked as a result of the military integration of development assistance in Afghanistan.
explanation: The text shows a coherent academic tone with concrete methodological details (panel-corrected standard errors, 34 provinces, 2010–2011), which is typical of human-written research; no obvious AI-generated patterns are evident.
